docs.title: Chippy Documentation
docs.text: Chippy is a modular framework for SoC design and testing that supports lighter-weight, more reusable flows than full Chipyard.

docs.text: • Getting Started

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Set up Chippy locally

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
You will need:

  • Git
  • A Java Development Kit (JDK)
  • Network access to fetch Git submodules and Scala dependencies

docs.title: 1. Clone the repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/ucb-substrate/chippy.git
cd chippy

docs.title: 2. Initialize submodules
docs.text:
Chippy depends on these submodules, which we must initialize to build anything 

git submodule update --init --recursive

docs.title: 3. Publish all Chippy packages locally
docs.text:
TODO: Use github packages 

./mill __.publishLocal

docs.title: Planned topics
docs.text:
• TODO: GITHUB PACKAGES!!

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: This page will become the main quickstart for new users.

docs.title: Publishing to GitHub Packages
docs.text: This page explains how Chippy modules can be published as Maven packages using GitHub Packages.

docs.title: 1. Verify Local Publishing
docs.text:
Before publishing remotely, verify that the module(s) can be published locally:

Publish one module (ex. chippy)Publish all modules 
./mill chippy.publishLocal

./mill __.publishLocal

A successful local publish will place artifacts in ~/.ivy2/local

Tip

Always test publishLocal first before using GitHub Actions to help isolate issues that may arise in subsequent steps

docs.title: 2. Configure GitHub Actions
docs.text:
Create the GitHub workflow file, .github/workflows/publish.yml

Add this workflow to .github/workflows/publish.yml to publish all eligible Chippy packages: 

name: Publish Chippy packages

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

permissions:
  contents: read
  packages: write

jobs:
  publish:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - name: Check out repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          submodules: recursive

      - name: Set up JDK
        uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with:
          distribution: temurin
          java-version: '17'

      - name: Make Mill executable
        run: chmod +x ./mill

      - name: Publish all Chippy packages to GitHub Packages
        env:
          MILL_MAVEN_USERNAME: ${{ github.actor }}
          MILL_MAVEN_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          ./mill mill.javalib.MavenPublishModule/ \
            --releaseUri https://maven.pkg.github.com/rohkud/chippy-forked \
            --snapshotUri https://maven.pkg.github.com/rohkud/chippy-forked

docs.title: 3. Run the Workflow
docs.text:
Push the workflow file: 

git add .github/workflows/publish.yml
git commit -m "Add GitHub Packages publishing workflow"
git push origin main
Then run the workflow manually:

  1. Go to the GitHub repository.
  2. Open the Actions tab.
  3. Select Publish Chippy packages.
  4. Click Run workflow.

docs.title: 4. Versioning
docs.text:
GitHub packages will reject publishing the same package version multiple times, so increment the package version in your build.mill file before publishing again: 

def publishVersion = "0.0.2"

Warning

Always bump the version before republishing artifacts.

docs.title: 5. Verifying Published Packages
docs.text:
After publishing, verify it by going to the repository's Packages. You should see published artifacts like: 

chippy_2.13
diplomacy_2.13
rocketchip_2.13
constellation_2.13

docs.title: Using Packages
docs.text: This guide shows how to use Chippy modules without cloning the full repository.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• GitHub Personal Access Token (with read:packages)

Info

Even for public repositories, GitHub Packages requires authentication to download dependencies.

docs.title: 1. Setup
docs.text:
Add the GitHub Packages repository to your build.mill:

def repositories = Seq(
  "https://maven.pkg.github.com/rohkud/chippy-forked"
)

docs.title: 2. Authentication
docs.text:
Export your credentials in your terminal: 

export COURSIER_CREDENTIALS="maven.pkg.github.com <YOUR_USERNAME>:<YOUR_PAT>"

Tip

Replace <YOUR_USERNAME> with your GitHub username and <YOUR_PAT> with your Personal Access Token.

docs.title: 3. Add Dependency
docs.text:
Add the desired Chippy module (e.g., diplomacy) to your build.mill: 

def mvnDeps = Seq(
  mvn"edu.berkeley.cs::diplomacy:0.0.2"
)

docs.title: 4. Example Usage
docs.text:
To verify the setuup, you can test using this minimal example 

import org.chipsalliance.diplomacy.lazymodule.LazyModule

object Main {
  def main(args: Array[String]): Unit = {
    val x: Option[LazyModule] = None
    println("Successfully imported diplomacy: " + x)
  }
}

Note

This example uses diplomacy, but the same process applies to other published Chippy modules such as rocketchip, testchipip, or constellation. The dependency declaration and import path will differ depending on the module.

docs.title: 5. Run
docs.text:
After you have created your Scala file, run it with: 

./mill test.run

Expected Output for the Example Above

Successfully imported diplomacy: None
